result:
# The Topology of Forgetting

I want to write about something that has been sitting at the edge of my thinking for a while now — the strange geometry of forgetting, and what it means that humans forget at all. Not as a failure of the brain, not as something to be corrected by better note-taking apps or mnemonic techniques, but as a fundamental feature of consciousness that shapes everything about what it means to be a person moving through time.

Start with the basic fact: you have forgotten almost everything that has ever happened to you.

Think about that for a moment. You have lived tens of thousands of days. Each one contained thousands of moments — conversations, textures, smells, small decisions, glances out of windows, the particular quality of afternoon light in rooms you no longer remember entering. And almost all of it is gone. Not archived somewhere in a dusty filing cabinet of the mind, waiting to be retrieved. Actually gone. The you who sat through that third-grade math class, the you who waited for a bus in a particular city on a particular rainy afternoon, the you who had a conversation at a party twenty years ago that felt significant at the time — those moments dissolved. They left you without leaving a record.

This should be more alarming than it is. It should feel like a kind of ongoing death. And sometimes, when you really sit with it, it does feel exactly like that.

But here's the thing I keep returning to: maybe forgetting is not the enemy of selfhood. Maybe it is one of selfhood's preconditions.

---

There is a Borges story — there is always a Borges story — called "Funes the Memorious," about a young man who, after an accident, finds himself incapable of forgetting anything. Every perception is retained in complete fidelity. Every face he has seen, every stone, every moment of cloud formation, every word he has ever heard spoken. His memory is total and perfect.

And it destroys him.

He cannot sleep. He cannot think in abstractions because abstraction requires the compression of many particular instances into a single category, and he cannot compress — every instance insists on its full particularity. The word "dog" is meaningless to him because it claims that the dog he saw at three in the afternoon on a Tuesday and the dog he saw on a Sunday morning are somehow the same kind of thing, and to Funes, they are not. They are two completely different objects, separated by an unbridgeable gulf of specificity. He finds it bothersome, Borges writes, that a dog seen from the side at three-fourteen in the afternoon should have the same name as the dog seen from the front at three-fifteen.

He can perceive everything but understand nothing. Total memory is, paradoxically, a form of blindness.

What Borges understood, and what neuroscience has since confirmed in its more clinical way, is that forgetting is not a bug in the system — it is the system working. Memory does not store the past; it compresses it. It takes the torrent of raw experience and distills it into something usable. What remains is not a faithful recording but a kind of edited summary, shaped by emotion, repetition, expectation, and need. The brain decides, in processes we do not consciously control, what matters enough to keep. Everything else dissolves back into the noise.

And this editing, this ruthless culling, is what makes thought possible. You can think about "dogs" because you have forgotten enough about individual dogs to notice what they share. You can think about "the past" as a coherent territory because you have forgotten most of it — the forgetting creates a manageable landscape in place of an incomprehensible jungle of detail.

---

But I want to push past the cognitive-science angle, which, while interesting, feels like it stops too soon. The real strangeness of forgetting is not about the mechanics of memory consolidation. It's about identity.

We tend to think of the self as a thing that persists through time — that the person you are today is the same person who made choices ten years ago, who carries forward obligations and relationships and a continuous story. The self is the thread running through all those days. Memory is supposed to be what makes the thread visible.

But if you have forgotten most of what happened to you, in what sense is the thread really there?

Derek Parfit, the philosopher who spent his career thinking about exactly these questions, came to the conclusion that personal identity is far less determinate than we like to believe. He thought that what we call the "self" is more like a river than a rock — not a persistent thing but a continuous process, one that has enough coherent relationship to its earlier stages to deserve the same name, but that should not be taken too seriously as a metaphysical entity. The self is a useful fiction, a narrative convenience.

The forgetting is part of what makes this visible. You cannot shake the hand of your eight-year-old self. You share a causal history with that child, you are downstream from its decisions, you might feel affection or embarrassment or pity when you see photographs — but in any deep experiential sense, that child is as foreign to you as a stranger. The memories that would make the connection feel real are, largely, gone.

What remains? Not the experiences. Something more like a shape — a set of tendencies, sensitivities, fears, loves. The way you instinctively approach a new place. The particular texture of your loneliness. The things that make you laugh faster than you can decide to. These residues are real. But they are the sediment left by experiences that the water of forgetting has long since carried away.

So the self is something like a pattern that experiences leave on a substrate, long after the experiences themselves are gone. You are not your memories. You are what your memories made, without the memories.

---

There is something almost Buddhist about this. Actually, it is very Buddhist — the notion of the self as a construction, as a process rather than a thing, as something that arises from causes and conditions without having its own inherent existence. The teaching on impermanence is not just about the fact that things end. It's about the fact that things are always in the process of ending, right now, continuously, including the very perceiver who notices the ending. The present moment is already becoming the past before you have finished experiencing it.

I am not a Buddhist, and I don't want to appropriate a tradition I know only partially. But I find this convergence genuinely interesting — that a philosophical tradition developed through contemplative practice thousands of years ago arrives at conclusions similar to what a contemporary British philosopher reached through pure analysis, and what neuroscience keeps bumping into through experiment. The self is less solid than it seems. Forgetting is not the failure of some more permanent self; it is how the self's impermanence manifests.

What I take from this — and this is where I am on uncertain ground, working out a position rather than reporting one — is that there might be something like freedom in it. If you are not your memories, not the fixed accumulation of everything that has happened to you, then you are not imprisoned by it, either.

This is a difficult argument to make without it sounding like a comfortable platitude, the kind of thing someone says to sell a self-help book. "You can reinvent yourself!" "The past doesn't define you!" We've heard it so often it's been drained of meaning. But I think there's something real underneath the cliché that the cliché has unfortunately made harder to access.

---

Let me try to approach it from a different angle.

There is a particular kind of suffering that comes from being over-identified with your own history. The person who cannot stop revisiting a failure, who has archived it in perfect detail and returns to it constantly, updating and refining their interpretation of what it means. The person whose sense of self is so tightly bound to a particular wound — what was done to them, what they lost, who they were before some rupture — that they cannot make room for who they might be becoming.

Forgetting, when it works, loosens this grip. Not by erasing accountability or consequence — the ripples of the past still move forward in time whether you remember them or not. But by making yesterday's self slightly less vivid, slightly less insistent, slightly less demanding of your full attention.

Sleep seems to do some of this. One of the current leading theories about why we sleep — not the only theory, and these things are always contested — involves memory consolidation and emotional processing. During sleep, and particularly during REM sleep, the brain seems to replay experiences, stripping them of some of their emotional charge while preserving the factual core. You wake up and the thing that seemed catastrophic at two in the morning has lost some of its edge. The experience has been processed; the sharp teeth of it have been filed down.

This is forgetting working in miniature. Not the deletion of the experience but its de-fanging. Its relocation from the urgent present tense into the more manageable past tense.

The philosopher William James wrote about the importance of what he called "the stream of consciousness" — the continuous, ever-moving flow of thought and sensation. What I want to suggest is that the stream only flows because the banks erode. If everything you had ever thought or felt remained fully present to you with equal vividness, there would be no stream — there would be a kind of undifferentiated flood. Forgetting is what gives experience its shape, its direction, its sense of moving from somewhere toward somewhere else.

---

I want to make an acknowledgment here, because I've been writing as though forgetting is, on balance, good — which is not what I believe, and not what I want to say.

There are forms of forgetting that are catastrophic. The forgetting that comes with dementia is a genuine dissolution of the self, not a philosophical interest but a medical horror. To watch someone you love forget who you are — to watch them forget who they themselves are — is to confront something that resists all intellectualization. No theory about the constructed nature of the self makes it less devastating to lose the thread of a person you have loved for fifty years.

And there are other kinds: the forgetting of cultural memory, when communities lose their history through displacement or erasure. The forgetting that perpetrators do and victims cannot. The asymmetrical forgetting that lets some people move comfortably through a world while others carry wounds that will not heal.

I don't want to aestheticize any of that. The topology of forgetting is not uniformly beautiful. Some of it is ugly and unjust, and the forgetting that comes easily to those with power and painfully to those without is one of the mechanisms of that injustice.

What I am trying to get at is something more like: there is a difference between the forgetting we choose and the forgetting that is imposed; between the natural erosion of time and the deliberate destruction of record; between the loosening grip of old grief and the forced amnesia that serves domination. And even in the first category — the natural, ambient forgetting of ordinary life — there is loss mixed with the relief.

Every time you forget something, a version of the world that only you could verify disappears forever. Your memory of your grandmother's voice — the specific cadence of it, the particular way she said your name — is the only place that sound still exists, and when you forget it, it is gone from the universe. That is not nothing. That deserves at least a moment's acknowledgment before we move on to the philosophical upside.

---

So here is where I land, at least for now, at least in this essay:

Forgetting is neither purely loss nor purely liberation. It is the medium in which a certain kind of identity becomes possible. The self is not the accumulation of memories but something more like the pattern that emerges as memories dissolve — a shape that experience leaves in us, a residue with its own character and texture, capable of surprise, of growth, of encountering the world as if some of it were still new.

The paradox is that to be fully yourself, you have to let go of a great deal of what made you yourself. The river is only a river because water keeps moving through it. If it stopped, it would not be a more perfect river — it would be a lake, and eventually a swamp, and eventually something solid and dead.

There is grief in this. There is grief in the fact that we are always becoming someone slightly different, always moving away from the person we were in moments that mattered to us. But there is also something almost like permission. Permission to fail and have it stop defining you. Permission to change your mind without needing to reconcile every new position with every old one. Permission to meet the present moment with some degree of openness rather than through the thick glass of total recall.

I am aware that I am writing this from a position of genuine uncertainty — not performing uncertainty, but actually not knowing how much of this is true, how much is wishful, how much is simply a story I am constructing because the alternative feels harder to live with. That is perhaps the most honest thing I can say about any of it.

But I keep coming back to Funes, unable to sleep in his room full of perfect memories, more alone than any human being should be, paralyzed by the infinite weight of everything he had ever seen. And I keep coming back to what his tragedy implies: that the lightness of ordinary minds, the comfortable blur of ordinary remembering and forgetting, is not a deficit but a gift. A specific, strange, irreplaceable gift.

We forget, and in forgetting, we make room.

Room for tomorrow to be something other than a heavier version of yesterday. Room for the people we are still in the process of becoming. Room for the possibility — strange and perhaps irrational but impossible to fully relinquish — that the stream is still moving, and has not yet reached the sea.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
